titleStmt.title: Letter from Maria Edgeworth to Margaret Ruxton, May 20, 1818
titleStmt.author: Maria Edgeworth
titleStmt.sponsor: Texas A & M University, University of Tennessee, Wake Forest University, Xavier University of Louisiana, Southern Methodist University
titleStmt.principal: Susan Egenolf, Meredith Hale, Hilary Havens, Jessica Richard, and Robin Runia

editionStmt.edition: First digital edition in TEI, date: June 4, 2026

availability.p: Reproduced by courtesy of Bodleian Library
availability.licence: Distributed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 (CC BY 4.0) Unported License

msDesc.head:
Letter from
Maria Edgeworth 1768-1849 71477273 62567029
to Margaret Ruxton 1746-1830

sourceDesc.bibl: In addition to our edited version, this letter was previously published in

editorialDecl.p: Edgeworth’s spelling and punctuation are retained in transcription, including non-standard spelling, line breaks, and hyphenation of words. The long s and ligatured forms are silently emended.

opener.date:
Spring-Farm
New Town Mt. Kennedy
May 20th 1818

div.p:
My dearest Aunt -- What I said in the
conclusion of my last letter to Sophy &
cannot exactly recollect but I perceive
that I have expressed myself horribly
ill since I have conveyed both to your
mind & hers a totally false & injurious
idea - The subject is so painful to me
and so dangerous since I explain
my feelings so ill that I am resolved
to say write not one word more about it -
only to assure you that I feel all your
tender solicitude for my happiness
and that "perturbed spirit" as I am &
as Sophy used always to call me I will
rest - rest -

As to the time of my coming to you Dear
estDearest aunt to that constant place of my
rest and refuge from care and sorrow
I cannot fise exactly till I see or hear
from my mother - She has not written
for some days because she is not able
to decide on her own motions till that
the Sneyds arrive in Town - By a letter
Honora recd. an hour ago it appears that
after having been delayed by Mrs. Sneyds
illness they were then actually setting out
- carriage at the door on the 13th- The rounda
boutroundabout journey to Sussex given up & to go straight
to London - I think my mother will be here
in about the 28th. - & will perhaps stay a week
or ten days including a day or two promised
to Miss Waller in Dublin - I will write again
the moment I know any thing more
positive - I merely write now to answer
your kindness as far as I can & that
you may not think me strangely silent.

Lady Lansdowne was so urgent for an
answer to her invitation to Bowood that
after delaying 3 weeks to write I thought
it wrong to delay longer & two days ago
I enclosed to my mother desiring a letter
to Lady L desiring her either to give or send
it to her Ladyship provided always she
approved of my plan .- I have told
Lady L that I cannot stir till I have com
pletelycompletely finished what I am writing &
till I have given time to my friends here
in Ireland to read & to form & impart their opinions
to me - My mother must read it - you
must read it at Black-Castle - and I believe
it should be read at Collon - This cannot
all be accomplished till before the end
of July - I have therefore begged Lady
L
would name any time after the
beginning of August that would be
most agreeable to her & have promised
then to accept her invitation to Bowood
- I have asked Honora to go with
me - and have felt great pleasure from
the affectionate manner in which she
as far as that
expressed her pleasure in the idea of a accom-
-panyingaccompanying me - Fanny has had her
share fully in London & England and as to none
of my younger sisters could it be so ad-
vantageousedvantageous
or so agreeable to go to
England at present as it must be to
dear Honora - To me it will be most
particularly agreeable to have her
with me & to feel in her trusting her
happiness to my care the strongest proof
of her confidence & affection - for she
is not a person who would go with
or toto any mortal she did not esteem &
love - she has written to my aunts -

As to modes of travelling - all that
is yet uncertain - If Sneyd & his Harriette
do not get a place in this neighborhood
Temple-Carrig - a castle in the air, whh.
has approached & reced[deletion]d[/deletion]ed alternately for
some weeks past & now depends upon
more ifs than I can enumerate or ex
plain,explain,
they will go to England - about
the time I should be ready to set out &
Sneyd & Mrs. E have said all that is kind
of the pleasure they should have in our
travelling together to Derbyshire - A brothers
convoy protection would be very agreeable - & wd.
prevent my feeling so much the want of that
without which I never stirred - But Lovell
Must go the 15th. that is earlier than I could
possibly go - He wd. I am sure meet us any
Where or do any thing that man or brother
could do to accomodate us at any inconve
nienceinconvenience to himself - But I know he has school-
schemes & excursions of his own in view
with which - exactly because he is so generous
& kind I should, hate dread his giving up for
our sake - Therefore I have never hinted to
him the slightest wish of his convoying us

- And we have a sheet anchor in Mrs. Billa
more who She is determined to go to England
to see her friends this summer - Travelling
with us would be an advantage to her in
point of economy & wd. be agreeable to all
parties - She will wait I am sure our conve
nienceconvenience - As to the look of the thing she is all
we could wish - As to the reality - at my age
& feeling no sort of difficulty in travelling
I should desire no more
My chief object in going to England is to
hear Francis Beauforts - Mr. Dumonts &
perhaps Mr. Wishaws opinions of The life
before it is published - Mr. Dumont I shall
meet - & very likely Mr. Wishaw at Bowood

- And at Sir Samuel Romilly's at Tanhurst
Where we shall probably go - Where & how
to meet Francis B - is not yet settled but
in the kindest manner he has long since
promised to meet me any where & convoy
me any where I please for this purpose

- As to my aunts & Byrkley Lodge that
is all in the great bosom of futurity -
a bosom not always as open to view -
- happily for mortals! - half hid & half reveal
ed imagination & hope are kept alive

5 -
Now my dearest Aunt I hope you will
approve so far of our schemes - I enclose
letters from Mr. Dumont - & Mrs. Marcet -
I am sure you will love Mrs. Marcet better
for this letter - it is so very amiable -
- I enclose the American lady's letter which
is partly interesting & partly tiresome -
- Return the letters as soon as you have
read them under cover to C J. Fox & Mangin
- but not to be forwarded to me - only to be kept
by Mr. Fox for me - He cannot now get franks
- Mr. Mangin has given warning of this to
all his friends - Mr. Vansettart wrote over &
made a great complaint of defalcation
of post office revenue - We had nothing to
do with it - but there were unreasonable
people who have brought on this suspension
of Mangin franks - Who I do not know

Though I cannot decide about my
own motions without till I know my
mothers wishes, yet I previously wish to
know yours my dear Aunt as to the
following points - Supposing the choice
in me - would you like to have me come
to you as soon as I probably can from hence
Dublin as I could & would in the coach
bringing with me the Ms. unfinished to go on
writing at Black Castle for a fortnight or 3 weeks
- Or wd. you rather that I should come to you
after I have finished my work for a fortnight, with Honora
on our
6 way to England - Your answer of course shall
be only for myself and I beg you to give it
me that I may be able to decide when I
speak know my mothers plans .

This house is far beyond my expec
tationsexpectations commodious & agreeable - It is an odd up-and-down steps house like an old There
English house - but with large sashes & panes & all clean papered & painted
are fine bedchambers besides 3 for men
servants in the offices - a delightful library
& agreeable sitting rooms - as to the number
of doors Edgeworths Town is nothing to this
house! - It is said that a carpenter who came
to view it lay awake a whole night afterwards
endeavoring in vain to count the number
of doors & make out the plan of ground plan
of the whole -

The beauties of the County of Wicklow, of which
I am told I have yet seen nothing astonish
and delight me - The more dear aunt
for reminding me often of Rostrevor -
But here is more bold in extent both of
the sublime & beautiful - T'will only
tell you what I have seen but not attempt
description or any thing but notes of
admiration !!!!!!!!!!!!!!! -

Drive from Bray Dublin to Bray -
Glen of the downs - drive to Powerscourt
church - Grattans - Powerscourt &c seen
only from the road - Lady Harriet Daly's
- seen only as we drove through to return
morning visit - Drove through Mr. Gunn's
place Mr. Kennedy - went to see Woodstock -
Immediately about this house is nothing
remarkably - tho' we shd. think it beautiful in
our flat pancake Cy. of Longford
The morning
that Lady Harriet Daly came here I had so vio
lentviolence a head ache that I could not go down to
see her - Met her at Powerscourt-Church -
very benevolent countenance - did not think
her so plain as I had been taught to expect
- Thought Lady Powerscourt beautiful -

- Sneyd is as kind & as happy as possible
- It is more impossible to be more affection
ate amiable & agreeable than Sneyd
is in the midst of his old & young brothers
& sisters in his own house - and Harriette
is so easy & so well bred & kind that we
feel as if we were completely at home
- She has had but little headache since
we came has been out every day in open-
carriage & enjoys our enjoyment of every
thing - The children are delighted from
morning till night - I should observe that
in this there is a poney here and an ass -
both which make a great figure in a
childs paradise - to say nothing of a
baruche seat - a jaunting car & the sea!
and shells on the shore - & boats inclusive

- There is no climax you will say in my enumeration or
perspective kept in my picture - No -
nor is there either in a childs a childr
the minds eye of childhood - Great and
little wonders & delight are only distinguished by
experience & comparison
Farewell dearest aunt - I forgot to tell you
8 that the first time I got upon the jaunting
car at this door being the second time I ever
was on one in my life, I thought I had sent
ed myself very well - Pakenham on half of
my cloak between me & Mrs. E - When lo! as
the man whipped his horse & set off, I _was
standing up I suppose but I cannot recol-
-lectrecollect how it happened - was as the carriage
turned thrown off & out - flat I found my
self on my back of my head on the gravel
my I was stunned but only for 1/2 a moment
- and my first returning thought was
"Well! my dear father was right - a jaunting
"car as he always said is the most dangerous
"vehicle in the world" - I jumped up, I fancy
from the spectators account as quickly
as I did when I tumbled over the back of
the chair for my uncle's amusement in
Baggot St. - But when I was up I saw! -
that Mrs. E and Pakenham were both
down lying prostrate on their backs a
yard from me - shockingly frightened I
was! Pakenham began to cry so I was
sure he was not dead that was one great
comfort - Mrs. E - could not stand at first
- But sat on the edge of the grass - however
she spoke & I saw there were no fractures
of limbs & I hoped more confusion than
contusion - we got on the car again & drove
on - Sneyd & Honora having trotted off some
time before on horse & poney - I felt a pain
in back of head & head ache but did my best
to laugh off my own & Harriette's ache & fright
9
- She was terribly frightened but I rather think not
much more hurt than I was - I had a headache
of course all day & was so violently sick at
night as would have alarmed me for the sake
of my poor brains but that I am used to such
sickness - and nothing bad came of it -
for either of us or for poor little Pakenham -
who had not a bruise even to boast of that I cdd
find - After this practical lesson on the
nature of centrifugal force - vis-insita
&c I have always sat still & held fast -
on a jaunting car - I was on it again yester
dayyesterday - but own - grandeur apart - I prefer
our own dear carriage which is as comfort
able as my father intended it should be -

Adieu - once more - If you ask how
or why Mrs. E or Pakenham followed me
out of the car I cannot tell nor can they
or any living creature who was present
- supposed that they were entangled in
the cloak - - But so it was - and there
it is - and here we are as well as ever

I have done very wrong indulge myself
in scribbling all this - don't encourage me - -
me - -
Mrsrs. Ruxton
